title: What a long bus ride taught me about patience
summary: Reflect on a slow journey — a long-distance bus, a delayed train, a mountain road — where the experience of having nothing to do changed how you understood patience.
topic: What a slow journey reveals about patience
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a slow journey (a long bus ride, a delayed train) and what it taught you about patience as something other than 'waiting without complaining'. Use the past perfect, present perfect, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: The bus from Quito to Tena was supposed to take six hours, and at the halfway point, when we had been stopped on a mountain road for nearly two of those hours, I noticed something about my fellow passengers that I had not expected…
If anyone had asked me beforehand to define patience, I would probably have given an answer about not getting angry, which is something I have now come to think misses the actual quality entirely…
Although delayed journeys are usually treated as time stolen from us, what I now suspect is that they offer the only kind of time that some lessons can be learned in, which is why…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nhịp điệu của sự chờ đợi
mất khái niệm về thời gian
sự bình thản của người già
khung cảnh nhìn từ cửa sổ
kiên nhẫn như một kỹ năng

hintVocab.phrase: to accept what you cannot change
hintVocab.meaningVi: chấp nhận điều bạn không thể thay đổi

sampleText:
The bus from Quito to Tena was supposed to take six hours. At the halfway point, when we had been stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours because of a landslide ahead, I started watching my fellow passengers more carefully than I had been watching the cliffs. A woman in her sixties had taken out a small woven bag, found a knife, and was peeling fruit for two small children she was travelling with. A man across the aisle had unwrapped a long bread roll and was eating it slowly. Three teenagers near the back, who I had assumed would be the first to grow restless, were quietly braiding each other's hair. No one had checked their phone in almost twenty minutes. I had spent most of the previous half hour silently calculating how much later I would now arrive at my hotel and rehearsing what I would say if dinner was already over.

What I had not noticed, until that moment, is that my fellow passengers were doing something I did not yet have the vocabulary for. They were not pretending the delay was fine. They were not distracting themselves from it. They were simply inside it, doing small ordinary things at the speed those things require, and that was visibly costing them nothing. Although I had always thought of patience as a kind of restraint — the holding back of irritation that I knew was underneath — what they were doing did not look like restraint at all. It looked like an absence of irritation, which is a completely different thing. The irritation was something I had brought onto the bus with me. They had simply not generated it in the first place, because they did not believe arriving an hour later was the kind of event their day was supposed to protect itself from.

What I have come to understand, slowly, since that journey is that patience is not really a virtue we apply to a slow situation. It is a relationship we have with time, and most of the time we have it badly. A person who treats every hour as something that can be either captured or lost will always feel cheated by a delay; a person who treats time as something that simply passes will not. However, this is not a recommendation to give up planning or to feel nothing about lateness. Some delays do matter. What I would say more carefully is that the kind of low-level irritation most of us feel at small delays is something we generate ourselves, and that watching people who do not generate it is one of the few clear ways to notice that the irritation is optional. The bus eventually moved, and I arrived at the hotel three hours late. Dinner was over. I ate bread and a banana in my room, and for the first time in years it did not feel like a small disaster.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect continuous in passive form: 'had been stopped'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We had been stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours' kết hợp dạng bị động ('been stopped') với quá khứ hoàn thành để diễn đạt một tình trạng đã kéo dài đến thời điểm chính. Dạng này phổ biến khi miêu tả các tình huống bị động trong tiếng Anh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We had been stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours' combines the passive ('been stopped') with the past perfect to describe a state that had lasted up to the main moment. This form is common when describing passive situations in English.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional in reported thought: 'rehearsing what I would say if dinner was already over'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Rehearsing what I would say if dinner was already over' lồng điều kiện loại 2 vào một suy nghĩ trong quá khứ. Cấu trúc 'would say if … was' giả định một tình huống chưa chắc chắn nhưng có thể đang xảy ra. Cả 'was' và 'were' đều được chấp nhận sau 'if' với chủ ngữ số ít trong tiếng Anh hàng ngày.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Rehearsing what I would say if dinner was already over' embeds a second conditional in a past thought. The structure 'would say if … was' supposes an uncertain situation possibly in progress. Both 'was' and 'were' are accepted after 'if' with a singular subject in everyday English.

sampleTranslationVi:
Xe buýt từ Quito đến Tena được dự kiến mất sáu giờ. Đến điểm giữa, khi chúng tôi đã bị dừng trên một con đường núi quanh co gần hai giờ vì một vụ sạt lở phía trước, tôi bắt đầu quan sát bạn đồng hành cẩn thận hơn cách tôi đang quan sát các vách đá. Một người phụ nữ ngoài sáu mươi đã lấy ra một cái túi dệt nhỏ, tìm thấy một con dao, và đang gọt trái cây cho hai đứa trẻ nhỏ bà đang đi cùng. Một người đàn ông bên kia lối đi đã mở một ổ bánh mì dài và đang ăn chậm rãi. Ba thiếu niên gần cuối xe, những người tôi đã cho rằng sẽ là người đầu tiên bồn chồn, đang lặng lẽ tết tóc cho nhau. Không ai kiểm tra điện thoại trong gần hai mươi phút. Tôi đã dành phần lớn nửa giờ trước đó âm thầm tính toán tôi sẽ đến khách sạn muộn hơn bao nhiêu và tập sẵn điều tôi sẽ nói nếu bữa tối đã kết thúc.

Điều tôi đã không nhận ra, cho đến khoảnh khắc đó, là bạn đồng hành của tôi đang làm điều mà tôi chưa có từ vựng để gọi tên. Họ không giả vờ rằng sự chậm trễ là ổn. Họ không tự đánh lạc hướng khỏi nó. Họ đơn giản đang ở trong nó, làm những việc nhỏ bình thường ở tốc độ những việc đó yêu cầu, và điều đó rõ ràng không tốn của họ gì. Mặc dù tôi luôn nghĩ về kiên nhẫn như một dạng kìm chế — việc kìm nén sự khó chịu mà tôi biết đang ẩn bên dưới — điều họ đang làm không hề trông giống kìm chế chút nào. Nó trông như một sự vắng mặt của sự khó chịu, đó là một thứ hoàn toàn khác. Sự khó chịu là thứ tôi đã mang lên xe với mình. Họ đơn giản không tạo ra nó ngay từ đầu, bởi vì họ không tin rằng đến muộn một giờ là loại sự kiện ngày của họ phải tự bảo vệ khỏi.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu, chậm rãi, kể từ chuyến đi đó là kiên nhẫn không thực sự là một đức tính ta áp dụng vào một tình huống chậm. Đó là một mối quan hệ ta có với thời gian, và phần lớn thời gian ta có nó kém. Một người coi mỗi giờ là thứ có thể bị bắt giữ hoặc mất sẽ luôn cảm thấy bị lừa bởi một sự chậm trễ; một người coi thời gian là thứ đơn giản trôi qua sẽ không. Tuy nhiên, đây không phải là gợi ý từ bỏ việc lên kế hoạch hay không cảm thấy gì về việc trễ. Một số chậm trễ thực sự quan trọng. Điều tôi sẽ nói cẩn thận hơn là loại sự khó chịu nhẹ mà hầu hết chúng ta cảm thấy với các sự chậm trễ nhỏ là thứ chúng ta tự tạo ra, và quan sát những người không tạo ra nó là một trong số ít cách rõ ràng để nhận thấy rằng sự khó chịu là tùy chọn. Xe buýt cuối cùng cũng di chuyển, và tôi đến khách sạn muộn ba giờ. Bữa tối đã kết thúc. Tôi ăn bánh mì và một quả chuối trong phòng, và lần đầu tiên trong nhiều năm nó không cảm thấy như một thảm họa nhỏ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses the past perfect passive?
options: We had stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours by police.
We had been stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours.
We have been stopped on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours at that point.
We had been stop on a winding mountain road for nearly two of those hours.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect passive = 'had been + past participle'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect passive = 'had been + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: No one has checked their phone in almost twenty minutes by then.
No one had check their phone in almost twenty minutes.
No one had checked their phone in almost twenty minutes.
No one had been check their phone in almost twenty minutes.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'check' is 'checked'.

options: Although I had always thought of patience as restraint, but what they were doing did not look like restraint at all.
Although, I had always thought of patience as restraint what they were doing did not look like restraint at all.
I had always thought of patience as restraint, although however what they were doing did not look like restraint at all.
Although I had always thought of patience as restraint, what they were doing did not look like restraint at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'come to understand' correctly?
options: What I have come to understand, slowly, since that journey is that patience is a relationship we have with time.
What I came to understand, slowly, since that journey is that patience is a relationship we have with time.
What I have came to understand, slowly, since that journey is that patience is a relationship we have with time.
What I have come understanding, slowly, since that journey is that patience is a relationship we have with time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly?
options: Three teenagers near the back, which I had assumed would be the first to grow restless, were quietly braiding each other's hair.
Three teenagers near the back, who I had assumed would be the first to grow restless, were quietly braiding each other's hair.
Three teenagers near the back, who I had assumed them would be the first to grow restless, were quietly braiding each other's hair.
Three teenagers near the back, who I had assumed would be the first to growing restless, were quietly braiding each other's hair.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who' for people in relative clauses; do not repeat the object with 'them'; 'to grow' is the correct infinitive.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' for people; no repeated object; 'to + base verb'.

options: Three teenagers were quietly braiding their own hair to each other.
Three teenagers were quietly braiding themselves' hair.
Three teenagers were quietly braiding each other's hair.
Three teenagers were quietly braiding hair each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Each other's + noun' (possessive form of reciprocal pronoun) is the correct collocation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'something we generate ourselves' correctly?
options: The kind of low-level irritation most of us feel at small delays is something we generate themselves.
The kind of low-level irritation most of us feel at small delays is something we generate us.
The kind of low-level irritation most of us feel at small delays is something we generate by us.
The kind of low-level irritation most of us feel at small delays is something we generate ourselves.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'one of the few clear ways to + base verb' correctly?
options: Watching people who do not generate it is one of the few clear ways to notice that the irritation is optional.
Watching people who do not generate it is one of the few clear ways noticing that the irritation is optional.
Watching people who do not generate it is one of the few clear way to notice that the irritation is optional.
Watching people who do not generate it is one of the few clear ways for notice that the irritation is optional.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One of the + plural noun + to + base verb' is the correct structure; 'ways' must be plural.
mcQuestions.hint: 'One of the + plural noun + to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'for the first time in years' correctly?
options: For the first time in years it did not felt like a small disaster.
For the first time in years it did not feel like a small disaster.
By the first time in years it did not feel like a small disaster.
On the first time in years it did not feel like a small disaster.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'For the first time in years' is the standard time phrase; past simple 'did not feel' is correct after 'did not'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'For the first time in + period'; 'did not + base verb'.

options: A person who treats every hour as something that can be both captured or lost.
A person who treats every hour as something that can be either captured nor lost.
A person who treats every hour as something that can be either captured or lost.
A person who treats every hour as something that can be either captured and lost.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Either + X + or + Y' is the standard structure; 'either … nor' and 'either … and' are wrong; 'both … or' is wrong (it should be 'both … and' for inclusive).
mcQuestions.hint: 'Either … or' for one of two alternatives.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'an absence of irritation, which is …'?
options: It looked like an absence of irritation, what is a completely different thing.
It looked like an absence of irritation, which it is a completely different thing.
It looked like an absence of irritation, that is a completely different thing.
It looked like an absence of irritation, which is a completely different thing.
mcQuestions.explanation: Non-defining relative clause uses 'which' (set off by a comma); do not use 'what' or 'that' here; no repeated 'it'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If much of the irritation we feel at small delays is something we generate ourselves rather than something the delay forces on us, what daily practices might quietly reduce that self-generated irritation — without becoming an exercise in pretending nothing matters?
